Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I'd try to set aside time in the evenings after my part-time job, but it rarely worked out consistently. I learn best by listening, so I'd often play lecture recordings while commuting or doing chores. When I could actually sit down to study, it was usually in bursts of a couple hours here and there rather than any structured schedule.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be consistent, maybe studying three or four evenings. But then work would pick up or I'd have family obligations, and suddenly I'm cramming everything into the weekend before a deadline. I tried to be regular, but life with limited resources doesn't always cooperate with good intentions.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about an upcoming deadline or realizing I'd fallen behind on something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything new posted, especially after getting an email about course updates. Honestly, anxiety about missing something important was a big motivator for logging in, even when I didn't have time to actually engage deeply.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester where checking the LMS became more habitual, usually first thing in the morning with my chai. I'd set a reminder on my phone to just open it and see what was there. But that routine fell apart when things got overwhelming with multiple courses and work. The mindfulness exercises they introduced actually helped me be more intentional about it for a while, but consistency was hard to maintain.
Q5: The recorded lectures were absolutely essential for me because I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them multiple times, pause, rewind, and really absorb the material. The discussion forums were also helpful when people asked questions I hadn't thought of—reading those threads clarified a lot of concepts. Anything I could listen to rather than just read was gold for me.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary reading materials and some of the optional practice quizzes. With limited time and 19 courses to juggle, I had to prioritize ruthlessly. The dense PDF documents felt overwhelming, and I knew I wouldn't retain information from just reading anyway. If it wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it usually got pushed to a 'maybe later' pile that never got addressed.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to either understanding a core concept or completing an assignment. If I could listen to it while doing something else, even better. I also valued anything that gave me quick feedback so I knew whether I was on the right track. Time is precious when you're working and studying, so efficiency matters a lot to me.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required ones had deadlines and consequences, so they got priority no matter what. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that would be nice to do' but it never made it to the top of the list. There's a guilt that comes with skipping them because you know they'd help, but survival mode means focusing on what's mandatory.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download the lecture audio files and listen to them on my phone during my commute and at work when possible. I also borrowed textbooks from friends and took handwritten notes that I'd review later. A lot of my learning happened through conversations with classmates over chai, talking through concepts verbally since that's how things stick for me.
Q10: There were many times I'd be listening to downloaded lectures while on the bus or walking, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also spend hours discussing course material with my study group in person—we'd meet at someone's house to save money on cafes. Some of my best learning happened through teaching concepts to my younger cousin, which obviously leaves no digital trace.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, maybe twice if the connection was good, then download the audio and never access it online again. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so offline studying became essential. I'd extract what I needed from the platform and then work with it independently, which I'm sure made my engagement look lower than it actually was.
Q12: Around midterms, everything crashed together—multiple deadlines, extra shifts at work because a colleague quit, and family health issues that needed attention. My attendance dropped and I couldn't keep up with assignments. The stress was overwhelming, and even though I tried the mindfulness techniques, I felt like I was drowning. That's when engagement became about survival rather than learning.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log into the LMS with good intentions but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Confusion about assignment requirements made me avoid starting them until the last minute. Competing responsibilities meant I was always triaging—what's most urgent, what can I afford to let slip. The LMS often lost that battle against immediate real-world demands.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be elsewhere, worrying about bills or tomorrow's work schedule. Sometimes I'd click through materials just to mark them as complete, knowing I wasn't really engaging. It's a hollow feeling, going through the motions while knowing you're not actually learning anything meaningful.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, especially the ones that seemed complex or required resources I didn't have easy access to. Part of it was avoidance because starting felt overwhelming when I was already stressed. I knew they were important, but the anxiety of not knowing where to begin made procrastination feel easier in the moment. The mindfulness sessions helped me recognize this pattern, but changing it was harder.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find time I didn't know I had. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the 48 hours before something was due. It's not ideal, but when you're juggling as much as I am, deadlines create the urgency needed to prioritize coursework over other demands.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. When I got encouraging feedback, it motivated me to engage more with that instructor's materials. But when feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose momentum and move on to other things. Honestly, by the time some feedback came, I'd already submitted the next assignment, so it felt disconnected from my learning process.
Q18: The course structure was challenging because it assumed a level of consistent availability I didn't have. Weekly modules with multiple components meant I was always catching up. However, having clear deadlines helped me prioritize. The structure would have worked better for me if there was more flexibility built in for students who can't engage in a linear, predictable way.
Q19: The audio and video content made engagement easier because they matched my learning style. What made it harder was when activities required consistent high-speed internet or assumed I had hours of uninterrupted study time. Group assignments were particularly difficult to coordinate with my unpredictable work schedule. More asynchronous options would have helped someone in my situation.
Q20: I think that describes me sometimes—lots of clicking around, watching videos, browsing discussions, but not submitting work. It could be that someone is genuinely trying to understand material but struggling to produce output. Or they might be overwhelmed and using activity as a form of productive procrastination, feeling busy without making progress on what actually counts.
Q21: That resonates with me too. I know people who barely touch the LMS but ace exams because they study from textbooks or have strong study groups. For me, my offline learning probably made my performance slightly better than my low engagement would suggest. The LMS only captures one dimension of how learning actually happens, especially for students who can't rely on constant connectivity.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they click on, time spent on pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the learning happening outside the platform. They can't capture listening to downloaded lectures, group discussions, or the mental processing that happens when you're thinking about course concepts during your commute. It's like measuring health only by step count.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, asking questions, making connections to things I already know. It's not about time spent on a platform but about mental investment in understanding. For me, engagement often looks like explaining a concept out loud to myself or debating ideas with friends. It's an active cognitive and emotional state, not just clicking through content.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually cared about learning despite my struggles, or the creative ways I adapted to study with limited resources. It wouldn't capture the conversations with classmates that deepened my understanding, or the hours spent listening to lectures while working. Most importantly, it wouldn't show my resilience—how I kept trying even when everything seemed stacked against me.
